committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade Hearing of evidence: Submitter list: Hon Todd McClay - Associate Minister of Foreign Affairs Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade Bede Corry, Chief Executive and Secretary of Foreign Affairs and Trade Taha Macpherson, Deputy Chief Executive – Policy Cecile Hillyer, Deputy Chief Executive – People and Operations Group Vangelis Vitalis, Deputy Secretary Trade and Economic Group Bernadette Cavanagh, Deputy Secretary Pacific and Development Group Grahame Morton, Deputy Secretary Americas and Asia Group Victoria Hallum, Deputy Secretary Multilateral and Legal Affairs Group Paula Wilson, Deputy Secretary Europe, Middle East & Africa and Australia Group Wayne Verhoeven, Chief Financial Officer
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade. Hon Todd McClay - Associate Minister of Foreign Affairs. Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade

chunks.text: Good morning. (coughing) (coughing) Please. (coughing) Not cheering. You need more seats. You'll need a bigger office. (laughing) Not as many, not as many as the Fients though. Oh. (laughing) Still coming. It's because it's the most interesting committee.

There's nothing else going on today. (laughing) Is the chair here? Someone, if they want to- Pull that chair up and take it to the floor. Sam, come off the table. Come sit with me. Here, if he wants. (laughing) Might be needed. Yeah, and I'll go down there and ask questions. (laughing) Next year.

Very good. All right. Good morning, Minister. Welcome along to the Foreign Affairs Defence Trade Committee. We are very excited to be conducting the annual review for the- (clears throat)... Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade today. Uh, and we welcome your presence with us this morning for 30 minutes to give us some oversight in your role as Associate Minister on behalf of the Minister, but obviously also as Minister of Trade and Investment too, I'm sure you have questions.

So, I'd like to hand over to you for some introductory comments and then there will be questions from members around the room. Thank you. Well, thank you Mr. Chair. Thank you. And committee members. I'm pleased to appear before the committee and I extend apologies from the, the, uh, Minister of Foreign Affairs and Trade, the Right Honourable Winston Peters, who's unable to join us today. Advancing and protecting New Zealand's national interests in this more contested, fragmented and volatile world requires determination and focus. Over the past year, the government has

responded to an increasingly challenging international environment with energy, resolve and with hard work. Uh, we have sustained a high tempo of international engagement, engagement at senior levels across government, focused on advancing the security and prosperity for all New Zealanders. Minister Peters' travel, along with that of the Prime Minister, the Minister of Defence, the Minister of Climate Change and myself has involved 88 high-level visits to 43 countries over the past year. This outward engagement was, um, supplemented by hosting

62 high-level visits to New Zealand by foreign leaders and ministers, showcasing the best our country has to offer and what we can offer to the world. Uh, collectively, this investment in building the international connections, uh, at a political, at a political level is a clear demonstration of our partners that New Zealand has contributed, has, uh, a contribution to make and a role to play on the global stage. These relations also set the platform for advancing the government's ambitious, ambitions across foreign policy trade and development

cooperation. Um, Mr Chair, the past year has seen strong delivery against priorities set out in the government's foreign policy reset. In the face of economic and geopolitical headwinds, uh, the foreign policy reset ensures our resources are directed, uh, are, are sent in the direction of efforts and are aligned in pursuing outcomes that matter to the security and prosperity of New Zealand. Notwithstanding these headwinds, headwinds, we have delivered real progress across all key lines of effort in the reset. Uh, priority relations in South and Southeast Asia

have been elevated through the comprehensive strategic partnerships with ASEAN as a whole and with Vietnam and Singapore. Uh, the relationship with India has seen a step change with enhanced political and defence engagement, and good progress has been made on the bilateral FTA negotiations following its launch in, only just in March of this year. Similar in North Asia, the relationship with the Republic of Korea has recently been bolstered with the conclusion of a comprehensive strategic partnership and high-level engagement with China, including the Prime Minister's visit

2025, has reinforced that important relationship. There's been increased effort in supporting resilient and prosperous Pacific demonstrated through delivery of over $517 million NZD in development cooperation funding in the year 24-25, aligned with our Pacific partners priorities, continued investment in Pacific regional institutions and humanitarian assistance, including following the December 2024 earthquakes in Vanuatu.

We have reinvigorated our relationship with like-minded partners, including through enhanced political engagement in Canberra, in London, Washington and Ottawa, sustained engagement in many lateral groups like the Indo-Pacific Four, and in contributions to collective security challenges, including supporting Ukraine's self-defence in the face of Russia's ongoing agression- aggression, following their illegal invasion.

We have targeted our multilateral engagement on global issues that matter most to New Zealand, including improving international stewardship of oceans, promoting reform of key multilateral institutions, upholding the Antarctic Treaty System, and contributing to global climate action and to disarmament and non-proliferation efforts. And we've strengthened our international trade architecture- (coughing)... in support of the government's goal of achieving a significant step change in export value over the next decade.

A new trade agreement with UAE, United Arab Emirates and an, and an updated ASEAN-Australia-New Zealand agreement entered into force, and negotiations were concluded with the Gulf Cooperation Council. Our focus on full implementation of recent trade agreements with the UK and the EU has delivered significant growth and exports into those markets, creating more opportunities and delivering greater returns for New Zealand.

And I want to recognise the support across the House for many of those agreements, including the early entry into force of the EU agreement and the significant benefits delivering for all New Zealanders, particularly exporters. We've also demonstrated leadership and creativity in working to sustain the international rules-based trading system, which remains under challenge, contributing to the establishment of the future investment and trade partnership, uh, and advancing opportunities for deeper engagement between the EU and the

CPTPP members. These outcomes all represent serious progress on the ambition of the reset and reflecting the strong leadership and direction the government is bringing to its international...... engagement. Um, the past year has also required resilience and agility to respond to pressing challenges beyond the reset priorities.

For example, emergency responses following natural disasters in the Pacific and arising from conflicts in the Middle East have required the evacuation of New Zealand citizens from dangerous situations offshore. In the trade and economic domain, the government has worked hard to support New Zealand businesses in navigating uncertainty, including through the impact of US tariffs. This will remain a priority and was positive, uh, it was positive to see a step in the right direction last month with the removal of additional tariffs on a range of New Zealand

agricultural, uh, products in, in the US. Being, um, positioned to manage uncertainty is the reality we face in a more challenged world. Advancing our priorities and defending our interests is a collective effort across government, including my cabinet colleagues and the broad range of New Zealand in- agencies who all play an important role in helping deliver on the ambition of the reset.

We enhance our influence and effectiveness in the work, uh, in the world by working collectively to address cross-cutting challenges and develop coordinated approaches, and by using all levers at our disposal. Uh, Mr. Chair and, uh, committee members, I thank you for the time today. I know that you have, uh, some hours before you, uh, with officials. I, uh, have, uh, um, the chief executive of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade, Bed Corry, here with me today, and the senior leadership team, uh, including Bengalis Vitalis, uh, who

will help, uh, me, uh, in the trade area. Uh, but yeah, I'm very happy to hear from you. Excellent. Thank you very much for that, Minister. Um, can I just clarify before we get underway, which two of the three Labour members are participating? No, no, usually I, I thought I'd been swapped out. That's right. No, I'll just sit here. I'm out.

Were you out? And Bernadette. Uh, Mr. Chair, I'm very relaxed about an extra member asking a question. Sorry- (laughs)... so you don't have to, you don't have to exclude somebody for my benefit. Very generous. Very generous. Okay. First question anyway, the Honorable Peeni Henare. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, Minister, officials. Um, thank you for your comprehensive remarks, uh, to open our session. You are right. Some of these, some of the more technical matters we can dive into, uh, with the officials.

My first question, uh, is the big move on Pacific reset, uh, and, uh, from the outset, it looks like it's the right thing to do. Yet what we're seeing across the Pacific, uh, far more challenges, uh, arising despite the huge efforts over the past two years, uh, with the Pacific reset.

I wondered if, Minister, you'd like to offer some views on that? Yeah. Well, the Pacific reset is very important. Uh, I, I would argue that, uh, one of, probably one of New Zealand's greatest obligations as a good neighbor and a friend is to our, you know, Pacific countries and particularly, uh, many of the smaller nations that face challenges around climate change.

And, you know, they're not immune to the geopolitical challenges that are going on around the world. Uh, we work closely with other countries, uh, advocating strongly, of course, for Australia, uh, with the US, with Europe, with most parts of the world about the importance of engagement with the Pacific and support them.

Uh, a lot of what we do is around the development, uh, space, uh, um, to support the nations, uh, uh, to help grow greater self-sufficiency. But we also need to recognize, you know, whilst geo- ge- geographical isolation in itself is a wonderful thing when you need peace and quiet, that the Pacific has not always been as quiet and peaceful as we are used to.

And so, we have continued to step up our efforts there. But maybe for greater detail about some of the activities in the, the reset either now or later on, officials could help that. Thank you. I appreciate that. Celia Abraham. Um, thank you. Um, following on your question from about the Pacific, um, I was interested, I've got some bigger questions, but I'd just like to focus on this given, um, the attendance of our colleagues from Vanuatu.

Yeah. Um, and I'd like to offer my sympathies for the earthquake almost a year ago. Um, and I wondered if you could enlarge or one of your, um, s- staff could enlarge on the humanitarian assistance to Port Vila and other areas there. Yeah. Well, thank you so much. Well, firstly, recognizing members of parliament here from Vanuatu, and thank you for, thank you for coming to New Zealand.

The relationship we have with Vanuatu is extremely important to New Zealand, not only to the, the, the government. Uh, and whenever there are challenges, uh, um, natural events, earthquakes and so on, New Zealand very easily quickly steps up to provide assistance. It's not a difficult decision to make, uh, funding losses is, uh, uh, uh, there available. Uh, actually, we believe as good neighbors and friends that we have an obligation to do that.

And, um, you know, the, the work that, uh, uh, New Zealand officials and others are doing is important. And I know directly from the Vanuatu government, it is welcomed and they're very pleased. And so, uh, you, you know, if I have a simple message for our Pacific neighbors is they don't have to worry about the support of New Zealands in time, New Zealand's in time of challenge and trouble. We have rights and obligations of both sides.

We, uh, you know, we address them respectfully as our equals, but they also have obligations to us and to themselves at the same time. But Bed, perhaps, um, sorry if there's a little bit more particularly on our efforts in Vanuatu. Well, thank you, Minister. Thank you, committee. And good morning.

Um, Bernadette may have further detail, but just to reaffirm the, the points that the minister has made, and we were, we were very, uh, quick off the mark to provide the, the help that was needed following that terrible earthquake last year. And it was in some ways a sort of a twofold crisis because obviously it affected our staff as well in our high commission, but also more, more tragically, um, impacted the people of Vanuatu. Uh, and there are, in a way, two stages to this.

There was the immediate response we had to provide...... which is the kind of things you can imagine you need immediately after an earthquake. But there is also a long tail of reconstruction, uh, that will ensue. Um, one, one good example, for example, is, is the damage done to the education sector across Vanuatu, the damage done to its, its schooling infrastructure.

And that's an area where New Zealand will be engaging in a multi-year, multimillion dollar program to help sustain that, that, uh, those, those vital schools, uh, to keep the children of Vanuatu educated despite the, the terrible plight the country is in as a result of the earthquake. Bernadette may have points to add. Yeah. Thank you very much. Good morning.

Um, and just very briefly to add, I think one of the interesting things out of this one was, A, I was getting in very quickly, um, and particularly with our, with our friends, Australia, so our friends, um, colleagues, to do the immediate assessment piece. But one of the things we did was have engineers up there very quickly from Beca who actually were able to do assessments through particularly the education sector and work out which buildings, school buildings were gonna be safe. So, they immediately had to be dealt with, which, which are the ones that we

will be dealing with over time. And actually, literally, uh, literally today, our high commission team are just moving into a, uh, a temporary, a longer term temporary premise for them, which just shows one year on of how challenging it has been on the ground with so much, um, so much infrastructure damage.

So, it was a, um, a, a tragedy and sadly, um, one that we have many times over in the Pacific. Thank you. Thank you. We share the ring of fire. Next question, Vanesh Walters. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And thank you, minister, and your officials for your work. Um, probably most recently the announcements in terms of supporting Ukraine and support for those impacted by Cyclone Detua as well. Um, I wonder if the, minister, if you've received or sought advice in relation to whether, uh, it's likely or not that we would be in breach

of our FTAs, any of our FTAs, should we fail to meet our Paris climate target? Well, the government, uh, has set out, uh, how it will achieve, uh, our obligation to Paris of net zero by 2050. And as I said earlier at the Minister of Climate Change earlier this year, that they, the good news is the, the, the, the pathway gets us there further than, uh, 2050. Uh, uh, we take our obligations extremely seriously in all international agreements, trade agreements as well as those on, uh, climate change.

And so ultimately, um, it's a hypothetical question as to the effect, uh, uh, it might have upon our trade under a number of free trade agreements because we are on track to do that. The one thing I would like to clarify though is that there, there is a, a confusion or misconception, uh, that says when we enter into agreement with another government on trade, you know, perhaps in the trade agreement, that that country, that government has authority to tell the New Zealand Parliament what to do.

If you look at those agreements, many of them are greater than aspirational, but it is very clear it is the sovereign right of the New Zealand Parliament to make decisions in the best interests of New Zealand. And how we meet those obligations is actually what we are, you know, going through and dealing with now. Y- you will have seen that the European Commissioner for Trade and Industry, uh, was in New Zealand just quite recently. Uh, we had a very big trade summit.

There were a lot of, uh, businesses from Europe that came down to see what more we could do together under the free trade agreement. He was asked the same question, it was only a week or so after the government had announced, uh, its new, uh, uh, um, policy on how the agriculture sector would meet its obligations and its contribution to New Zealand being net zero by 2050.

He, to the media and subsequently in other engagement, said he had seen the changes that New Zealand had made, he believed they were compatible with the free trade agreement, uh, and that New Zealand meets its obligations. And it was for us to decide how to do that. And so, uh, uh, uh, there is no advice that I have based on the decisions that have been made, uh, that were contraventions of the, any agreement. And the reason for that is the government takes their obligations seriously, and would not make a decision that would contravene the current free

trade agreement. So- Just very quick sup- And thank you for that, uh, that explanation. I agree that it's up to New Zealand to decide how we meet those targets. Should we fail to do so, however, is there legal advice, advice in terms- Well-... of what our potential liability is? Well interestingly, there's two parts of the debate. The first is our obligation under Paris, uh, to be net zero by 2050.

The second is there are domestic targets set by the last government. Unfortunately, when the previous government set those targets a- around NDCs in 2030, it was already impossible at that time to meet them. And so actually, hypothetically, uh, that was a question that might have been asked by a previous cabinet.

However, the very good news is we are on target, on track to meet an obligation by 2050. And therefore, to suppose what would happen if we didn't is not an important discussion to have because we remain on track. Supplementary from- No, not... It's the new primary. Thank you. Oh, okay. Sorry. We'll come to Tim Costly first. Yeah. My question's also on trade agreements.

Um, we've seen a, a lot of progress I think in the year, but this one, uh, we had the Gulf States agreement signed. But I think also there'd be a lot of interest in hearing about India and progress there. Are you able to- Yeah.... um, touch on sort of free trade for the year in review, but also with an eye to the future?

Yeah. Well, I suppose more broadly to start, that New Zealand's a trading nation. You know, it's often said 400 million people around the world get 10% of their diet from New Zealand. Uh, we produce more food and fiber than we could consume ourselves. It is of very high quality. It has some... You know, New Zealand producers have some of the highest quality reputation or best reputation of anywhere in the world.

And the world and consumers are demanding this. With that comes a story of provenance. So, in everything the government does, current and previous governments, it is about upholding that reputation. But the world is not a fair place. People don't have to buy from us. And so- Mm-hmm. So ultimately, the government's job is to level the playing field. We are blessed to have, uh, I would say arguably the best trade negotiators in the world.... and particularly, you know, for our slides, uh, and I know, uh, uh, that's the envy of many

other ministers that I go up against to talk about trade and we negotiate against. You know, New Zealand has, uh, around 100 free trade, uh, double tax or investment agreements with countries of the world, which levels the playing field, and it gives real opportunity for New Zealand businesses to consider risk and where they want to put their own effort rather than the government telling them what they should do.

So, our job is to level the playing field. Uh, well, you know, I was very pleased that we were able to reach across the House and get the UFTA in force many, many months sooner than had been predicted, and we did that because, one, trade should be bipartisan. It doesn't mean we have to agree on everything, but we must agree trade access is good for New Zealand.

And if we don't do that, then fundamentally, we don't understand how the New Zealand economy works, and we miss opportunities to tell New Zealanders that we wholeheartedly, as a Parliament, will back them on the world stage, that EUFTA having entered into force early, uh, in the first 18 months has been, uh, worth an extra $2 billion worth of exports from New Zealand, that largely would not have happened without that agreement. Uh, and secondly, uh, the EU- the UKFTA has seen a very significant increase in exports over the two years. The, uh, trade agreement with the UAE, which was

the fastest negotiated- Yeah.... uh, uh, by our officials in the history of New Zealand on the highest quality on entry to force now has entered into force, and it opens up huge opportunities, a $500 billion, uh, market for New Zealand. You mentioned the GCC. That's concluded. It's yet to be signed. The, the member countries are going through the process of, uh, getting permission to sign. We expect that will happen- Yeah.

... in due course. And with India, um, uh, you know, against, uh, uh, a lot of, um, concern that in a, you know, a, a negotiation might not be able to take place, talk about priority, whether India should be a priority or not, I was very pleased with the Prime Minister in March of this year. Only eight months ago, we were able to launch negotiations.

There has been significant engagement across the board from both sides. You would have seen I hosted the, the Indian Trade Minister- Yeah.... to New Zealand and have just been back there, uh, uh, recently, and I expect one more trip to India before the end of this year. We are making progress and making good progress, but there remains a lot of work to do because I will only come back to the Prime Minister and Cabinet and say, "We should conclude if it is meaningful and a good deal for New Zealand."

And supplementary if I, if I can. So, c- d- d- do you have an example you can give us of what that ... Like, you talked about the UAE and that coming into force earlier, in fact, so early I think we, we beat this year in review. But, um, the, the, the Gulf states, the UAE, uh, U- the EU, now working on India. What- what's the tangible deliverable that gives to New Zealand, those agreements, and, and the immense workload that goes in from MFAT- Yeah.

... and, and others to deliver? Well, it's a huge amount of work on the part of MFAT just both during the negotiation, but, but equally as importantly, post, uh, conclusion to socialize and promote the agreement. There's this very unusual thing happens in trade. Uh, when two governments say they're gonna, want to negotiate a trade agreement, generally trade starts going up.

Mm-hmm. Uh, when they conclude, even if it's not implemented, trade goes up. And then, of course, when it's i- cl- included in force, it can't... That's without us doing anything. But what we know is when we do go and put an effort, MFACT go around the country holding seminars and providing information, then exponentially the opportunity through trade is more significant.

In the first year of the UKFTA, I don't remember exact figures so don't hold me to them, but whilst our trade increased, we found 30 to 40% of exporters were not using the new FTA with preferential access. And largely that may be because they weren't aware of it or aware of how. And so rather than saying they're wrong, it's our job to help, uh, get out there and, and pull them into it, because generally New Zealanders benefit. W- w- y- you know, there's a lot of information being provided, and why don't I leave it to officials later and when you, we talk to them about some of

the statistics. But it is simple as that. If you take the UKFTA, the EUFTA, uh, the start of the, the UAE one, the incus- increase in trade in the GCC, and we have seen an increase in trade in India since the Government started talking so positively about that- Mm-hmm. ... the tangible ge- benefit for New Zealanders is more jobs, higher-paying jobs, and greater economic certainty.

You know, when I go out on the world stage with my officials, I go out there not to fix the problems of the world, to deliver a good deal for Kiwis, to back them so that they can do well in New Zealand and continue to do well internationally. Thank you, Peter. Excellent. Next question. Celia Wadburn. Thank you. Um, in the annual report, there's, um, some mention of humanitarian aid and evacuations on- for Gaza, and there's some mention of ministerial, um, statements. I would like to know, um, what advice was given to the Government about recognition of

Palestine? Um, what were the main arguments and evidence presented by MFAT, um, in favor of recognition, and how were they evaluated? Yeah. Look, a lot of that information has been released already, but at a higher level, um, uh, um, you know, the Government has considered this very, very carefully. There have been a wide number, a growing number, of statements on behalf of the Government, from the Prime Minister, and from the, uh, Minister of Foreign Affairs, including in our Parliament, uh, uh, talking about the great tragedy of what

has happened in Gaza. We have been calling for enduring peace and the need for peace o- on both sides. Cabinet have very carefully weighed up, uh, uh, the pros and cons of, of, uh, this issue. Uh, and, uh, I want to be very clear, as I think the Minister of Foreign Affairs has when he made a very detailed, uh, statement in the United Nations of New Zealand's position, it is not a matter of if, if it is a matter of when, uh, New Zealand would recognize, uh-... Palestine, but the conditions are not there at the moment for it to meet the test in international

law. It is of each country to make that decision for itself, but we take our responsibilities very carefully. But in as far as a bit more detail, perhaps I'll pass to, uh, Bee Gluhrman. Okay. Did you- W- Well, no, no, I'll hear from you and then I've, uh, a couple more sups, unless you, you may answer them as well.

Sure. Okay. Thank you. So we provided, we provided advice on three options, effectively, to the minister. And qu- as the minister said, quite a lot of this is now available on the MFAT website 'cause we've done a proactive release so that you can see the back and forth between us and the minister's office on this. We provided advice on, on recognition, on recognition with conditions, and on non-recognition.

None of those options were straightforward in our eyes. All of them carried risks, and our advice tended towards recognition. But this has, this is the minister's paper. The minister has to be comfortable with the recommendations in it for him to take it to cabinet. And in the back and forth with the minister's office, he made it clear that recognition, straight-up recognition, wasn't something that he supported because, again as the minister said, there are too many questions about exactly what the future government might

look like. Hamas is still there. Hamas hasn't disarmed. There's big questions about whether the Palestinian Authority is ready to step up and govern all of the occupied Palestinian territories or whether there has to be some alternative to that. So at the time, um, at the time the advice was that there were three different options the minister w- could go with, and the minister decided to proceed with non-recognition at that time.

Well, just to clarify, it was a cabinet decision. Yes, indeed it was. Absolutely. Yeah, yeah. And as the minister said, we still believe in the right for Palestinians to self-determine their future and we think that having two states sitting side by side would enable both populations to live in peace, but we're just not at that point at the moment.

And subsequently, what, or maybe leading up to the decision, what reports or advice did you receive from, um, our friends and allies, uh, Western countries that did recognize Palestine? They- What, what, what, you know, what has been the impact on our relationships- Yeah.

... with other countries that- Mm-hmm.... you know, Canada, France, et cetera, that did? Mm-hmm. Well, the Minister of Foreign Affairs engaged very widely on a number of issues, uh, both here in New Zealand and as he has traveled. I know he's had extensive conversations with various countries. Some of that, uh, would be better for him to talk about at another stage rather than, than me, I think. Uh, but at the same time that the cabinet took a decision, uh, uh, I, uh, as I have chaired ...

(indistinct) ... has talked directly to other governments about our position and the reason for it. Uh, you know, if you think about a few of the countries that you mentioned, we have had and continue to have very strong and important relationships with them. Uh, we have the ability to not always agree on everything a- and to have the ability both publicly, uh, and, uh, uh, you know, directly have conversations about the reason for these things, uh, that, that there may be a different position that the governments, uh, takes.

And that's a very important thing when it comes to foreign affairs. I've often in the past in opposition said, you know, megaphone, meg- megaphone diplomacy from Wellington where we're shouting at people but yet delivering, trying to deliver a message domestically doesn't often work well. New Zealand is respected for being considered both in foreign affairs and trade and the decisions that we take, and we're at our best when we're out talking to others, explaining the position and having an effect upon, uh, you know,

international affairs. Thank And would it, would it be fair to say, Minister, that there was a strong focus through that time on actually trying to improve the humanitarian situation on the ground, and recognition in and of itself wouldn't achieve that, whereas actually delivering aid, for example- Yeah, that's right.... more tangible for all. Well, we have made-... uh, significant, significant contributions, uh, to aid and support, uh, to the area, uh, for it to, to go to the Gaza.

Uh, we remain very concerned about the humanitarian effect that, uh, you know, that has happened there, uh, against civilians. Uh, we have made that clear, uh, both, uh, in Parliament through statements in the UN and in other forums around the world, and we feel we have a, uh, continued role to play.

But I think the most important thing here is that for those countries in the world that have made a decision to recognize, they are no different from New Zealand in as far as there is not a government there in Palestine to work with, and that is one of the important things for New Zealand that when we make the decision, and again it's not a matter of if, it's a matter of when, that we are able to engage that it is a legitimate government, it is not a terrorist organization, uh, and that, uh, we will be able to uphold the very high standards New Zealand always has had in

recognition of statehood. Can I ask- Could the Ministry... I think we might have- Next question is from, supplementary from Venushi Walters. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Of course. Under international l- law, New Zealand has obligations under the Genocide Convention as a third party. We also have the ICJ advisory opinion in relation to the occupied territories that I think has about five pages worth of, um, information on what third parties should do. In light of that, what advice, if any, has the minister sought about New Zealand's obligations under both the

convention and the decision? Look, I, I think the Minister of Foreign Affairs has made statements as well as answered questions in the House quite extensively on that. I don't have a lot more to add other than, you know, the clarity he has delivered directly to Parli- Parliament on a number of occasions other than say we take our international obligations very seriously, including as a result of our engagement with other organizations. Uh, I'll ask, uh, Bee maybe to comment with the proviso that in some instances, information or advice we're

given is very, very carefully and strictly legally privileged. But apart from that, I'll pass to Bee. Thank you, Minister. I mean, I, I wasn't sure whether the question was drawing a linkage between, um, any obligations under, uh, the Genocide Convention or pursuant to ICJ decisions and recognition, um, but I, I don't think we would draw a direct red cross between those two.

No. So, I may have- Just-... misunderstood your question. Y- to clarify, not in terms of recognition, but procurement, visas, ensuring that there's no third party information sharing that would breach our obligations. So not recognition. I mean, I think as a statement of general principle, we would always uphold our international obligations. Um, there may be particular points of the law that I... that in a sub- the subsequent session, um, I could invite Victoria Hallam, our, our government's chief international legal advisor, to advise

on. But we, we, as a, you know, it's enormously important to New Zealand always to uphold the international obligations that it subscribed to. We do got time for one quick supplementary question. Yeah, just on the advice that the Ministry provided on recognition, has the Ministry provided that previously in the last few years? And in particular, I think about May 2021 when there was a motion before the House, uh, on this matter- Mm-hmm.

... did it provide the same advice at that time? That's a good question, and I'm not sure I can answer it, I'm afraid. It's preceded my time. We provided the advice before September this year because there was going to be a dedicated session discussion it, discussing it at the UN General Assembly, and we wanted to make sure that we were in a position- But at that time, there was, before the New Zealand Parliament- Mm-hmm.
